stem: Consider the sequence defined by \( a_n = \dfrac{[[p]]n + ([[q]])}{[[r]]n + ([[s]])} \) for \( n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: For a ratio of two linear expressions in \( n \), divide numerator and denominator by \( n \): \( a_n = \dfrac{[[p]] + ([[q]])/n}{[[r]] + ([[s]])/n} \to \dfrac{[[p]]}{[[r]]} = [[answer]] \) as \( n \to \infty \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( a_0 \), the ratio of the constant terms, not the limiting ratio of the leading coefficients.
This inverts the ratio, taking denominator-leading over numerator-leading.
This adds the coefficients within each term instead of keeping only the leading (highest-degree) coefficients.
This pairs the numerator constant with the denominator leading coefficient, mixing the terms.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Consider the sequence \( a_n = \sqrt{n^{2} + [[a]]n + [[b]]} - n \) for \( n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: Multiply and divide by the conjugate: \( a_n = \dfrac{([[a]])n + [[b]]}{\sqrt{n^{2}+[[a]]n+[[b]]}+n} \). Dividing numerator and denominator by \( n \), the denominator \( \to 2 \), so \( a_n \to \dfrac{[[a]]}{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2 after rationalizing.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
This uses the constant term \( [[b]] \) instead of the linear coefficient \( [[a]] \).
This adds both coefficients instead of taking half of just the linear one.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Consider the sequence \( a_n = \dfrac{[[A]]\cdot [[r]]^{n} + ([[B]])}{[[C]]\cdot [[r]]^{n} + ([[D]])} \) for \( n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: Factor \( [[r]]^n \) out of numerator and denominator: \( a_n = \dfrac{[[A]] + ([[B]])/[[r]]^{n}}{[[C]] + ([[D]])/[[r]]^{n}} \). Since \( [[r]] > 1 \), the terms \( [[r]]^{-n} \to 0 \), so \( a_n \to \dfrac{[[A]]}{[[C]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of leading coefficients.
This is one more than the correct limit.
This is one less than the correct limit.
This doubles the correct limit.
This halves the correct limit.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.

stem: Consider the sequence \( a_n = [[c]] + \dfrac{[[A]]n + ([[B]])}{[[r]]^{n}} \) for \( n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: An exponential with base \( [[r]] > 1 \) grows faster than any polynomial, so \( \dfrac{[[A]]n+[[B]]}{[[r]]^n} \to 0 \) as \( n \to \infty \). Thus \( a_n \to [[c]] + 0 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the fraction tends to a nonzero constant rather than 0.
This wrongly assumes the fraction tends to \( -1 \) rather than 0.
This mistakes the numerator's growth for surviving in the limit.
This drops the additive constant \( [[c]] \) entirely.
This is off by 2 from the correct limit.
This is off by 2 from the correct limit.

stem: Consider the sequence \( a_n = [[c]] + \left([[A]]n^{[[k]]} + [[B]]\right)^{1/n} \) for \( n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: For any polynomial growing only polynomially in \( n \), the \( n \)-th root tends to \( 1 \): \( \left([[A]]n^{[[k]]}+[[B]]\right)^{1/n} \to 1 \) as \( n\to\infty \) (take logs: \( \tfrac{1}{n}\ln([[A]]n^{[[k]]}+[[B]]) \to 0 \)). So \( a_n \to [[c]] + 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the \( n \)-th root tends to 1, not 0, leaving out that contribution.
This mistakes the leading coefficient \( [[A]] \) for the limit of the root.
This mistakes the root's index \( [[k]] \) for the limit of the root.
This is one less than the correct limit.
This is one more than the correct limit.
This ignores the additive constant \( [[c]] \) entirely.

stem: Consider the sequence \( a_n = [[c]] + \left(1 + \dfrac{[[a]]}{n}\right)^{n} \) for \( n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: This is the classic definition of the exponential: \( \left(1+\dfrac{a}{n}\right)^n \to e^{a} \) as \( n\to\infty \). With \( a = [[a]] \), \( a_n \to [[c]] + e^{[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakes the base-limit form for a linear approximation \( 1 + a \) instead of \( e^a \).
This uses \( e^{0}=1 \), forgetting the exponent \( [[a]] \) entirely.
This drops the exponential term completely.
This flips the sign of \( [[a]] \) in the exponent.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Consider the sequence \( a_n = [[A]]\,n \sin\!\left(\dfrac{[[B]]}{n}\right) \) for \( n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: Let \( x = [[B]]/n \to 0 \). Then \( a_n = [[A]]\cdot\dfrac{\sin x}{x}\cdot [[B]] \), and \( \dfrac{\sin x}{x}\to 1 \) as \( x\to 0 \), so \( a_n \to [[A]]\cdot [[B]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly treats \( \sin([[B]]/n) \) as vanishing fast enough to send the whole product to 0, ignoring the \( n \) factor.
This drops the factor of \( [[B]] \) coming from the small-angle substitution.
This drops the factor of \( [[A]] \) coming from the leading coefficient.
This adds the coefficients instead of multiplying them.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Consider the sequence \( a_n = [[A]]\,n^{2} \left(1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{[[B]]}{n}\right)\right) \) for \( n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: Let \( x = [[B]]/n \to 0 \). Using \( \dfrac{1-\cos x}{x^{2}} \to \dfrac12 \), write \( a_n = [[A]]\cdot\dfrac{1-\cos x}{x^{2}}\cdot [[B]]^{2} \to [[A]]\cdot\dfrac12\cdot [[B]]^{2} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This wrongly assumes the whole expression vanishes, forgetting the \( n^2 \) factor balances the \( 1-\cos \) decay.
This forgets the factor of \( \tfrac12 \) from the standard \( (1-\cos x)/x^2 \to 1/2 \) limit.
This uses only the leading coefficient, dropping \( [[B]]^2 \) entirely.
This uses \( [[B]] \) instead of \( [[B]]^2 \), as if the limit were degree 1 in \( B \).
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.

stem: Consider the sequence \( a_n = \dfrac{[[A]] \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} k}{n^{2}} + [[c]] \) for \( n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots \). Evaluate \( \displaystyle\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n \).
solution: By the Gauss sum formula, \( \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n}k = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \), so \( a_n = \dfrac{[[A]](n+1)}{2n} + [[c]] \to \dfrac{[[A]]}{2} + [[c]] = [[answer]] \) as \( n\to\infty \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the sum term entirely, as if it vanished relative to \( n^2 \).
This treats the sum as growing like \( n \) rather than \( n^2/2 \), overcounting by a factor of 2.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This is off by one from the correct limit.
This doubles the coefficient's contribution instead of halving it.
This flips the sign of the sum's contribution.
